sentences.text: Pilati.
Yeah.
P-I-L-A-T-I.
And I would like to tell you a little bit more about the contact between the church and our group.
And I'm going now back-- earlier times.
Not always did we have the best contact with the church.
And the, the relationship between our group and local priests were on a very individual basis, which means sometimes a local priest was very friendly to us, and wanted to help him.
But his community, or his-- how should I say community-- didn't make it possible.
But in Nonantola, there was a different wind.
The church under Monsignor Pilati's, perhaps, leadership could get the population to help us.
And perhaps also because the population there was under the finger of the local fascists, and they didn't like the local fascists, they were more likely to help us.
For example, the farmers.
They took us in, open arms.
And I don't want to say they were very well-educated people.
That was the, the poorest of farmers you can imagine.
They didn't have anything at all.
But yet, they shared whatever they had, what little they had, with us.
Perhaps the local church pushed them a little bit.
You know?
They made them aware of our distress.
Perhaps the other people didn't, but they did.
And this is the reason why I want to give them so much credit.
Under the most difficult circumstances and the most harsh conditions under their fascistic rule and under the occupation now from German, from Germans, they did help us.
And there was another person I want to mention.
His name is Pacifici.
Chichibo is his name.
It's very hard for me to spell.
He lost his life helping us.
Chichibo was an Italian Jew, which came with Dr.
Jaccia and the dottoressa to our group, to Villa Emma, as an connector to the Italian government.
He spoke fluently-- Italian, of course, he was an Italian Jew-- but he could also speak German.

So whenever we needed anything and we couldn't get it through the, through the fascists, or the [NON-ENGLISH] or through the priests, or through the doctor, he was the, he was the person in the middle.
He was the contact between the DELASEM and us.
And now comes also the time where we have to escape, because the small children, were in a cloister.
And the nuns and also the priests told us, we would do everything for you, but this is temporary.
This is a shelter of temporary nature.
No, the name I don't know.
But I know in Nonantola there is only one big basilica, which exists today.
And left to the basilica is a large cloister, which still exists today the same way it was when I left.
Most probably the nuns are not there anymore.
But the nuns exist and the school of the novices exists today because I visited them.
But the names I don't-- I cannot, I cannot tell you.
That was the point when they told us, look, temporarily we can take us.
Temporarily the younger-- the older boys can stay with the farmers.
But now that the Germans are here, sooner or later, somebody will talk.
Somebody will denounce you.
And we cannot take the chance.
You have to leave.
And again, the Jewish pipeline works.
And we found out that the only [NON-ENGLISH] open-- the only way open, out, was to go to Switzerland.
Yeah.
Chichibo was sent to us from the DELASEM at the same time we took over the 30 or 38 children from the Yugoslav group from Split as additional help to communicate with the Italian authorities.
At the time when we came to Nonantola, none of us spoke Italian.
None of them in Italy, in Nonantola, spoke German.
The communication was very, very difficult.
There were a few Jews in Modena which spoke Jewish-- Yiddish, you know, so we could communicate.
So immediately the DELASEM sent Dr.
Jaccia, which spoke Italian and German, as an middle man.
Dottoressa came to us for medical, you know, supervision, and Pacifici Chichibo came also to help us.
They didn't stay with us.
So when the time-- when we had to flee, when we had to go to the farmers, when the children went into the cloister, it was made to us to understand the nature that is very temporary.
At the same time we found out that the only way out is Switzerland.
I forgot to mention something to you.
Between Nonantola and Modena an British prisoner of war crime existed.
And whenever we were in the fields, we sometimes were in contact with British officers, and sergeants, and military personnel, which were under the supervision of Italian third grade troops.
You know, old, old soldiers, not really-- and they were very lax.
So we told them a little message, and they threw a little message back.
And even chocolate we got, the first time in years.
The small children got chocolate from the British and American soldiers which were imprisoned.
When Mussolini were imprisoned and Badoglio tried to make armistice, at that time, they opened the gates of this prison camp.
And all the Americans and British soldiers, which were in the military prisoner of war camp, escaped in the countryside.
And they tried to make their way either south to the, to the frontier, where the fighting was, and cross over to their troop, to their side.
Or they tried to do the same thing what we did, go to Switzerland.
Some of the older boys joined this group and went south.
And a few managed to cross the German lines into the British lines and American lines and actually made it to Palestine in 1942 when the war was going on.
A lot of those prisoners, I told you, where they camp was opened up, made it through and we went to the alliance.
But I have to mention one boy, because he should get recognition, too.
His name is Max Federman.
And Max Federman was my age.
And he was a native of Frankfurt, Germany.
And Max Federman went down south to reach the British-American forces, but didn't make it.
He made it to the partisans in the Apennine.
And he was one of the most recognized partisan fighter in the Italian war.
And he got-- after the war-- medals from the Italian government.
And the American government recognized him and gave him a preferred quota to emigrate in 1945 to the United States.
So I want to recognize Max Federman.
He lives in California today, too.
But again, to come back to our situation, we knew we had to escape to Switzerland.
Joschko sent Pacifici Chichibo to Milano, and from there, to Como Chiasso to the Swiss border, to find out the possibility of an escape route to Switzerland.
At that time, you must understand, we are talking now '43, October '43, the American and British forces bombed the Reggio Emilia area where we live continuously.
If it was bomb, bombing by day, we knew it is the Americans.
If it was bombing by night, we knew it is the British, because they had like a train schedule going.
And not only our area was bombed-- I don't want that you think only we were bombed-- they were bombing, on their way to Germany, overflew us.
Train-- trains between Modena and Milan were running, but not on a scheduled basis.
All the passenger cars were taken from Italy and shipped to Germany, because they needed them there.
The railways were bombed out.
So what they did for-- in Italy, they had either open, open cars, or just, you know, the most true cars available.
And they used them for the civilian population.
And when Chichibo traveled, and later on when we traveled, we traveled in open cars or in just cars which were for animals or something like that.
And we didn't know where the train really goes.
And we didn't know when it will go, but we knew the direction it goes.
And this railway goes to-- the next place is Reggio Emilia, and the next place is this and this.
And finally, the end of the line is Milano.
And we didn't know if it would take one day or two days, or--
Now we were-- good that you mention adults, because Jaccia and all the Italians left us.
They went home.
They didn't go into the cloister.
The only person which stayed with us was Chichibo.
He was the only one.
And, of course, my mother, and you know, but we were the refugees.
So we tried to find a way to go to Switzerland.
And contacts at that time were very, very limited.
Whatever contact we had with Switzerland was broken off, because now we were in hiding.
We had no way of getting messages.
We decided to follow him.
We have to split up.
Arnold Weineger, Edgar Asher, his sister, and me, and Chichibo, we came together with Joschko.
Joschko came out of the cloister, came to the farmers, and we had a little what is called [NON-ENGLISH].
We talked about it and we decided we will to try to find a way to Switzerland.
We will try to find the possibility of sending money to buy the smugglers.
And we will give you the direction how to do it.
And on one day we left, and we went to Milan.
Like I told you, on the way we were bombed in Reggio Emilia.
The train was stopped on one side of the city.
We had to walk to the other side of the city because the railway was just-- it was bombed out.
The train couldn't continue.
And on the other side of the city they had another train waiting and we continued.
And we finally made it to Milano.
And finally we made it to Como Chiasso.
In Como Chiasso Chichibo found out that by Varese, Ponte Tresa, there is a possibility to cross the River Tresa.
And the River Tresa is quite a large river, perhaps 20, 30 feet wide.
But on one point there is an underwater dam.
An underwater dam is stone construction where the top is perhaps three feet wide.
But a foot under water on one side is a deep lake, a deep-- the water is dammed up so that it is a pretty deep area.
And the other side, water falls over the dam, perhaps 15 feet, 20 feet down to the river.
I don't know exactly the reason why the dam is there, but perhaps it's a water control dam or something like that.
But that is the only way somebody could cross the river, because one side of the river is Ponte Tresa, the Italian city.
And on the other side is Switzerland.
When we found this out, we decided that Chichibo will go back and notify Joschko.
And Arnold Weineger, Edgar Asher, his sister, and me, we crossed and we went to Switzerland.
Now not going the same way--
No, I just want to say-- we couldn't get this way, so we went over Como Chiasso through the woods.
And we choose a different way.
Because this way we wanted to preserve for the children.
And we made our way from Como Chiasso to Lugano.
Before we left, we got a few Swiss franc, which we obtained in Nonantola in an illegal way of getting money.
And we traveled from Lugano to Zurich, where we had a contact with the Jewish community for many, many years by the name of Dr.
Lichtheim.
And when we came to Zurich, the main train station-- three boys and one girl-- we didn't know where it is.
And there were Swiss police around and Swiss soldiers.
And we approached an old woman, and we asked her for the address.
And she looked at us, and she said-- I will never forget it, in very heavy Swiss-German dialect-- [NON-ENGLISH].
That means you're dirty [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, you're dirty people from somewhere else or something like that.
But she showed us the way how to go to Dr.
Lichtheim's address.
So as bad as she seemed to be, she was helpful to us.
And Dr.
Lichtheim kept us for a few days, made contact with the smugglers Chichibo found in Varese, and sent money.
Because at that time they wanted 1,000 lira per person to smuggle to the way where the dam is. 1,000 lira wasn't so much because the Italian money wasn't the very best.
But multiply it by 100.
That was a large sum of money, even for the-- Do you have any idea how he made contact with Varese?
With Varese?
In Switzerland you could travel free.
I want to tell you.
They, they-- Switzerland was completely surrounded by Germany.
And Switzerland, at that time, was very German-friendly.
Because they knew if they wouldn't be, they would be overrun by the next day.
So Germany used Switzerland as a depository for money, depository for spies, for precious metal, and for all kind of things.
And the Italians had smugglers going back and forth between Italia, Italy, and Switzerland, and to smuggle either food or cigarettes.
And Switzerland let this be, because they needed certain things, too.
They were completely surrounded by Germany.
The Swiss Jews had free travel.
They were Swiss nationals.
They were very, very, wealthy, very, very rich.
They had all kind of money coming from the United States and from South America to help Jews, so money was never an object there.
The object was how to get them in and how to keep them.
The Jewish community in Switzerland was very small and never put any pressure on the Swiss government.
And at the same time, Switzerland had a very German-friendly attitude and actually returned Jewish refugees on the border, taken on the border, directly into German hands.
How?
Well, again, perhaps only hearsay.
But a wonderful film was made in Switzerland for their own people.
The film name is And it's an old archives, and it tells the story of people returned into the hand of the Germans.
To come back, we stayed three, four days.
The money went to Ponte Tresa, to the smugglers, into the hands of Chichibo.
And this we know because Joschko received the notification, Chichibo came back to Nonantola and told him, now you bring-- now you can bring the children.
At the time, Joschko was perhaps 26, 27.
Now our time-- we couldn't stay longer in the home of Dr.
Lichtheim.
We had to go to the Swiss Fremdenpolizei and represent us, which we did.
And at first, they couldn't believe that we made it to Zurich, because we told them we came from Italy.
And he said, how come you were not caught in Lugano, in the Italian part?
So after a while they interned us in one of the worst internation-- intern camps in the German part of Switzerland by the name of Busserach.
That was near Solothurn and Basel.
And--
The Swiss police concerned with foreigners-- Fremdenpolizei.
That's a special group of Swiss police under the Swiss government, of course.
Now I have to come back to Chichibo.
Chichibo received the money after a few days' wait.
And he decided to make it back to Joschko to bring the children.
And can you imagine?
He made their way back to Como Chiasso to Milano on those terrible train rides back.
And he had arrived in the beginning of October 1943, back into the, in the cloister of Nonantola.
At that time, nobody from the more adult teenage children, which were hiding by the farmers, existed anymore, because they took off and went south to the American and British lines.
Then Joschko got the notification from Chichibo that the way is open.
He made an emotional goodbye to the nuns, and to the priest, and to Dr.
Bocari.
And he decided to take the children, including my mother and Mr.
Avien, our cook for many years, and make the way from Nonantola to Modena, and from Modena to Milano, and from there to Como Chiasso and Varese by train.
Well, first of all, my mother survived and she came with me to, later on, to Palestine, to Israel, and finally, to the United States.
Oh yes, she told me the story.
And, of course, I told this-- I got the story told many times from Joschko.
Directly, and from the children.
And I met the children later on in Switzerland.
At this time, I was interned in Busserach with Edgar Asher, and Arnold Weineger, and [NON-ENGLISH], another fella.
Four people, four boys were interned in Busserach under the most brutal and hard condition you would never have expected to be in Switzerland.
